---
title: "Your Manuscript Title Here"
# NOTE: AMA limits titles to 25 words.
# ---- Authors: uncomment for non-anonymous PDF output ----
# author:
#   - name: First Author
#     email: first@university.edu
#     affiliations:
#       - name: University Name
#     attributes:
#       corresponding: true
#   - name: Second Author
#     affiliations:
#       - name: Another University
# author-note: >
#   First Author is Professor of Marketing, Department Name, University Name
#   (email: first@university.edu). Corresponding author. Second Author is
#   Assistant Professor of Marketing, Department Name, Another University
#   (email: second@university.edu).
anonymous: true
# Set anonymous: false to show authors and author-note in the PDF.
# Author info never appears in the DOCX (which follows AMA submission format).
abstract: |
  Replace this text with your abstract. The abstract is limited to 200 words
  and summarizes the key components of the manuscript, offering the reader a
  sample of the manuscript. The abstract should be written in third person
  (e.g., "the authors" rather than "we").
# NOTE: AMA limits the abstract to 200 words, written in third person.
keywords:
  - keyword one
  - keyword two
  - keyword three
# NOTE: Include up to 8 primary keywords.
# ---- JPPM Only: Uncomment the following for Journal of Public Policy & Marketing ----
# policy-contribution-statement: |
#   Succinctly articulate (1) the policy conversation that the submission
#   initiates or to which it seeks to contribute, (2) how the manuscript
#   moves understanding beyond existing marketing and public policy
#   literature, and (3) what specific policy stakeholders might be impacted
#   by the results of the inquiry and how. Max 300 words.
format:
  amaquarto-docx: default
  amaquarto-pdf:
    pdf-format: article
    # pdf-format options:
    #   article    - Single-column publication-style layout (default).
    #   manuscript - Single-column submission-style layout (12pt,
    #                double-spaced, 1-inch margins). Matches DOCX format.
    keep-tex: true
bibliography: bibliography.bib
---

<!--
  AMA SUBMISSION GUIDELINES REMINDERS
  ====================================

  FILE ORGANIZATION:
  - File 1: Title page (see title-page.qmd) -- contains author info,
    acknowledgments, and statements/declarations.
  - File 2: Main document (this file) -- title, abstract, keywords, main text,
    references, and appendices. NO identifying author information.
  - File 3 (optional): Web Appendix (see web-appendix.qmd) -- uploaded as PDF.

  FORMATTING:
  - DOCX (submission): 12pt Times New Roman, double-spaced, 1-inch margins,
    no page numbers/headers/footers (all handled by the extension).
  - PDF (article): 11pt, single-column, centered page numbers.
  - PDF (manuscript): 12pt, single-column, double-spaced, 1-inch margins
    (matches DOCX submission format, rendered as PDF).
  - Page maximum: 50 pages inclusive of all content (applies to submission).

  HEADINGS (do not number sections):
  - Use #, ##, ### in Quarto markdown. Formatting is automatic per format:
    DOCX/manuscript: H1 centered bold; H2 flush left, bold italic; H3 italic.
    Article: H1 large bold italic; H2 bold italic; H3 italic.
  - Use Title-Style Capitalization for H1 and H2.
  - Use Sentence-style capitalization for H3.
  - Do not use more than three heading levels.
  - Do not label the opening section as "Introduction."

  TABLES AND FIGURES:
  - Place within the text, not at the end.
  - Number consecutively. Refer by number (e.g., see Table 1).
  - Table titles above, figure titles below (AMA standard).
  - Titles should reflect the takeaway.
  - Report actual p-values (three digits), not asterisks with thresholds.
  - Include standard errors in tables.
  - Figures: use Arial font, label axes, include error bars.
  - Use ::: {.table-notes} for notes below tables/figures.

  CITATIONS:
  - In-text: (Author Year) or Author (Year).
  - Up to three authors: list all names; four or more: first author et al.

  FOOTNOTES:
  - Use sparingly; no more than 10 footnotes, max 40 words each.
-->

<!-- Do NOT label this opening section as "Introduction." -->

Social media has become an essential part of daily life, transforming the way
consumers communicate and interact with others. Social media has also changed
the way brands connect with consumers. With the rise of social media
influencers, many brands use them as a marketing strategy to reach and engage
with their target audiences. Social media influencers are individuals who have a
large following on social media platforms and are viewed as experts in a
particular niche. They are seen as trusted sources of information and can
influence the purchase decisions of their followers.

# Literature Review

Brand loyalty is an essential aspect of marketing, as it represents the degree
to which customers are committed to purchasing a particular brand. Brand loyalty
is important because it leads to repeat purchases and positive word-of-mouth,
which can lead to increased sales and profitability for the brand.[^fn1]

[^fn1]: Footnotes should be used sparingly (i.e., no more than 10 footnotes)
and must be concise (i.e., not exceeding 40 words).

## Social Media Influencers and Brand Loyalty

This study aims to investigate the role of social media influencers in enhancing
brand loyalty among millennial consumers. The study focuses on millennial
consumers because they are the most active users of social media and are
considered an important target audience for brands.

| Variable | *M* | *SD* | *n* |
|:---------|:---:|:----:|:---:|
| Brand loyalty | 4.21 | 1.02 | 200 |
| Purchase intent | 3.87 | 1.15 | 200 |
| Influencer trust | 4.56 | .89 | 200 |

: Descriptive Statistics for Key Variables. {#tbl-descriptives}

::: {.table-notes}
*Notes.* Standard deviations in parentheses. All variables measured on
7-point Likert scales.
:::

### Influencer marketing as a strategy

Social media influencers can influence the purchase decisions of their followers
by promoting products or services on their social media channels. Influencer
marketing has become a popular marketing strategy among brands. Prior research
has demonstrated the effectiveness of such strategies
[@lafreniere2022; @ozturan2022].

## Millennial Consumers

Millennial consumers are an important target audience for brands because they
are the largest generation in the workforce and have significant buying power.
Several studies have investigated the role of social media influencers in
enhancing brand loyalty among millennial consumers.

# Methodology

A mixed-methods approach was used to collect data, including an online survey
and in-depth interviews. The research design follows established practices in
the marketing literature [@gupta2023; @sridhar2023].

## Data Collection

### Survey design

Describe the survey instrument, sampling strategy, and data collection
procedures here.

## Analysis

The data were analyzed using hierarchical regression analysis. In the first
step, we entered the control variables (age, gender, income, and education
level). In the second step, we entered the main effects. In the third step, we
entered the interaction terms.

Equation 1 presents the baseline model specification:

$$
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \epsilon_i
$$ {#eq-baseline}

Note that AMA style uses roman (upright) font for variables and does not place
a zero before decimal points. For example, the coefficient was significant
(*p* = .012, SE = .34).

| | Attitude | | Purchase Intent | |
|:--|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| **Predictor** | **Model 1** | **Model 2** | **Model 1** | **Model 2** |
| Intercept | 2.45 (.21) | 2.31 (.20) | 1.98 (.24) | 1.82 (.23) |
| Influencer trust | .42 (.08) | .38 (.08) | .35 (.09) | .31 (.09) |
| Brand familiarity | .28 (.07) | .25 (.07) | .22 (.08) | .19 (.08) |
| Trust x Familiarity | | .15 (.06) | | .12 (.07) |
| *R*^2^ | .31 | .34 | .24 | .26 |

: Hierarchical Regression Results. {#tbl-regression}

::: {.table-notes}
*Notes.* Standard errors in parentheses. Actual *p*-values reported;
no asterisk thresholds used (per AMA policy).
:::

# Results

The results showed significant main effects on attitudes toward the brand and
purchase intentions. The findings are consistent with prior theoretical
predictions.

# General Discussion

Summarize findings and discuss theoretical and managerial implications.

# Appendix A: Stimuli {.appendix}

This appendix presents the stimuli and scenarios used in the experiment.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
